---
title: "MAT 142: Day 2 -- Foundational Algebra"
author: Dr. Gilbert
format: 
  revealjs:
    smaller: true
date: today
date-format: long
theme: serif
incremental: true
---

```{r global-options, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)

theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 16))
```

```{css}
code.sourceCode {
  font-size: 1.3em;
  /* or try font-size: xx-large; */
}

a {
  color: purple;
}

a:link {
  color: purple;
}

a:visited {
  color: purple;
}
```

## Reminders

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

At our last meeting, we discussed each of the following:

:::

+ Order of operations and combining like terms.
+ Evaluating expressions by plugging in values for variables.
+ Expanding products of variable expressions.
+ Using rules for exponents.

:::{.fragment}

Try the following warm-up problems.

:::

:::{.fragment}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="33%"}

**Problem 1:** Simplify $4x^2 - 3x + 7 - 2\left(x^2 + 5x -1\right)$

**Problem 4:** Simplify $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(x^3 y^2 z^{-1}\right)^2}{\left(x^2y^{-1}\right)^{-3}}}$

:::

:::{.column width="30%"}

**Problem 2:** Evaluate $2x^3 - x + 4$ for $x = -2$

**Problem 5:** Expand and Simplify $\left(x + 3\right)^2 - \left(x - 3\right)^2$

:::

:::{.column width="33%"}

**Problem 3:** Expand and simplify $\left(2x - 3\right)\left(x^2 + x - 4\right)$

**Problem 6:** Simplify $\displaystyle{\frac{3x^4y^2 - 6x^2y^3}{3x^2y^2}}$

:::

::::

:::

</div>

## Objectives

:::{.fragment}

Today, we'll focus on factoring, rational expressions, and simplification.

:::

:::{.fragment}

After today's class meeting, you should be able to:

:::

+ Identify the greatest common factor from a collection of terms.
+ Factor the greatest common factor out of a collection of terms.
+ Factor a quadratic expression.
+ Factor a difference of squares. 
+ Use a common denominator to add or subtract two rational expressions.
+ Simplify complex fractions.
+ Use factoring to reduce complex fractions.

## Factoring: Greatest Common Factor

:::{.fragment}

Factoring will be a main strategy for us as we seek to find roots and solve equations. As with last class meeting, we'll do a few problems for review but, if you need a more comprehensive review, [check out this video](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 1:** Rewrite the expression $6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

\begin{align} 6x^4 + 8x^3 -10x^2
\end{align}

:::

## Factoring: Greatest Common Factor

Factoring will be a main strategy for us as we seek to find roots and solve equations. As with last class meeting, we'll do a few problems for review but, if you need a more comprehensive review, [check out this video](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 1:** Rewrite the expression $6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \color{blue}{6}x^4 + \color{blue}{8}x^3 - \color{blue}{10}x^2
\end{align}


## Factoring: Greatest Common Factor

Factoring will be a main strategy for us as we seek to find roots and solve equations. As with last class meeting, we'll do a few problems for review but, if you need a more comprehensive review, [check out this video](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 1:** Rewrite the expression $6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \color{blue}{6}x^4 + \color{blue}{8}x^3 - \color{blue}{10}x^2 &= \color{blue}{2}\left(3x^4 + 4x^3 - 5x^2\right)
\end{align}

## Factoring: Greatest Common Factor

Factoring will be a main strategy for us as we seek to find roots and solve equations. As with last class meeting, we'll do a few problems for review but, if you need a more comprehensive review, [check out this video](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 1:** Rewrite the expression $6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} 6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2 &= 2\left(3\color{blue}{x^4} + 4\color{blue}{x^3} - 5\color{blue}{x^2}\right)
\end{align}

## Factoring: Greatest Common Factor

Factoring will be a main strategy for us as we seek to find roots and solve equations. As with last class meeting, we'll do a few problems for review but, if you need a more comprehensive review, [check out this video](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 1:** Rewrite the expression $6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} 6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2 &= 2\left(3\color{blue}{x^4} + 4\color{blue}{x^3} - 5\color{blue}{x^2}\right)\\
&= 2\color{blue}{x^2}\left(3x^2 + 4x - 5\right)
\end{align}

## Factoring: Greatest Common Factor

Factoring will be a main strategy for us as we seek to find roots and solve equations. As with last class meeting, we'll do a few problems for review but, if you need a more comprehensive review, [check out this video](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 1:** Rewrite the expression $6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} 6x^4 + 8x^3 - 10x^2 &= 2\left(3\color{blue}{x^4} + 4\color{blue}{x^3} - 5\color{blue}{x^2}\right)\\
&= \boxed{~2x^2\left(3x^2 + 4x - 5\right)~}
\end{align}

:::{.fragment}

**Try It 1:** Rewrite the expression $12x^3y^2 - 8x^2y^3 + 4xy$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It 2:** Rewrite the expression $3x\left(x+2\right) - 7\left(x+2\right)$ by factoring out the greatest common factor.

:::

## Factoring: Quadratics I 

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 2:** Factor the quadratic $x^2 - 7x + 12$, if possible.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

+ Start by looking at the leading/quadratic term (that's the one with $x^2$)
    + Since the leading term is just $x^2$, your factors will take the form $\left(x ~?~ \text{___}\right)\left(x ~?~ \text{____}\right)$
+ Next, look at the sign in front of the constant term (that's the one with no variables in it)
    + If the sign is positive, then both constant terms in the factors will have the same sign
    + If the sign is negative, then the constant terms in the factors will have different signs
    + Here, they'll have the same sign!
+ Now, the sign on the linear term (the one having $x^1$) will tell us what the sign on those constant terms will be
    + We'll have $\left(x - \text{___}\right)\left(x - \text{___}\right)$
+ To finish off, we'll need two factors that multiply together to give us $12$, but add together to give us $-7$
    + This means $x^2 - 7x + 12 = \boxed{~\left(x - 4\right)\left(x - 3\right)~}$

:::

</div>

## Factoring: Quadratics II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 3:** Factor the quadratic $x^2 + 9x - 36$, if possible.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

+ Start by looking at the leading/quadratic term (that's the one with $x^2$).
    + Since the leading term is just $x^2$ again, your factors will take the form $\left(x ~?~ \text{___}\right)\left(x ~?~ \text{____}\right)$.
+ Next, look at the sign in front of the constant term (that's the one with no variables in it)
    + If the sign is positive, then both constant terms in the factors will have the same sign.
    + If the sign is negative, then the constant terms in the factors will have different signs.
    + Here, they'll have different signs!
    + We'll have $\left(x + \text{___}\right)\left(x - \text{___}\right)$
+ Now the sign in front of the linear term (that's the one with $x^1$) will tell us what sign the dominant (bigger) number must have
    + Here, the bigger number will be positive
+ To finish off, we'll need two factors that multiply together to give us $-36$, but add together to give us $9$
    + This means $x^2 + 9x - 36 = \boxed{~\left(x + 12\right)\left(x - 3\right)~}$

:::

</div>

## Factoring: Quadratics III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 4:** Factor the quadratic $6x^2 + x - 15$, if possible.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

+ Start by looking at the leading/quadratic term (that's the one with $x^2$ in it)
    + This time the coefficient is not $1$, so it's less clear how to move forward.
    + The leading term in each factor will be a linear term in $x$ -- that is, it will be a constant times $x$, and those two leading terms must multiply together to give us $6x^2$.
    + Our factors will be of the form $\left(\text{___}x ~?~\text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}x ~?~\text{___}\right)$
+ Next, look at the sign in front of the constant term (that's the one with no variables in it)
    + If the sign is positive, then both constant terms in the factors will have the same sign.
    + If the sign is negative, then the constant terms in the factors will have different signs.
    + Here, they'll have different signs!
    + We'll have $\left(\text{___}x + \text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}x - \text{___}\right)$
+ Now, we need to fill in $\left(ax + b\right)\left(cx - d\right)$ so that $ac = 6$, $bd = 15$, and $bc - ad = 1$.
    + This means $6x^2 + x - 15 = \boxed{~\left(3x + 5\right)\left(2x - 3\right)~}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Note:** This one was quite difficult and we'll have opportunities to avoid this type of factoring if we want. You should, however, work to become comfortable with the strategies from **Problem 2** and **Problem 3**.

:::

</div>

## Factoring: Quadratics IV

:::{.fragment}

The next scenario can be handled with the same strategy we used for the previous three examples.

:::

:::{.fragment}

It is a special case though, which warrants becoming familiar with.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Definition (Difference of Squares):** An expression of the form $a^2 - b^2$ is known as a *difference of squares* and it factors nicely into $\left(a + b\right)\left(a - b\right)$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 5:** Factor the difference of squares $x^2 - 16$, if possible.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* $x^2 - 16 = \boxed{~\left(x + 4\right)\left(x - 4\right)}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 6:** Factor the difference of squares $9x^2 - 25$, if possible.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* $9x^2 - 25 = \boxed{~\left(3x + 5\right)\left(3x - 5\right)}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Note:** While the difference of squares factors nicely, a sum of squares will not.

:::

## Factoring: Quadratics Practice

:::{.fragment}

Try each of the following practice problems.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It 3:** Factor the quadratic $x^2 + 3x - 18$, if possible.

**Try It 4:** Factor the quadratic $x^2 - 5x - 24$, if possible.

**Try It 5:** Factor the quadratic $6x^2 + 18x - 24$, if possible.

**Try It 6:** Factor the quadratic $4x^2 -9$, if possible.

**Try It 7:** Factor the quadratic $x^2 + 36$, if possible.

**Try It 8:** Factor the quadratic $2x^2 + 7x + 3$, if possible.

**Try It 9:** Factor the quadratic $4x^2 - 4x - 3$, if possible.

:::

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

:::

:::{.fragment}

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{\color{blue}{6}} + \frac{x}{\color{blue}{15}} &= \frac{x}{\color{blue}{2\left(3\right)}} + \frac{x}{\color{blue}{5\left(3\right)}}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15} &= \frac{x}{\color{blue}{2}\left(3\right)} + \frac{x}{\color{blue}{5}\left(3\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15} &= \frac{x}{\color{blue}{2}\left(3\right)} + \frac{x}{\color{blue}{5}\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{\color{blue}{5}x}{\color{blue}{5\left(2\right)}\left(3\right)} + \frac{\color{blue}{2}x}{\color{blue}{2\left(5\right)}\left(3\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15} &= \frac{x}{2\left(3\right)} + \frac{x}{5\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{5x}{5\left(2\right)\left(3\right)} + \frac{2x}{2\left(5\right)\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{5x}{30} + \frac{2x}{30}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15} &= \frac{x}{2\left(3\right)} + \frac{x}{5\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{5x}{5\left(2\right)\left(3\right)} + \frac{2x}{2\left(5\right)\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{5x}{30} + \frac{2x}{30}\\
&= \frac{5x + 2x}{30}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

Rational expressions are expressions of the form $\displaystyle{\frac{\left(\text{First Expression}\right)}{\left(\text{Second Expression}\right)}}$

You might think of these expressions as "fractions" which may contain variables.

As with previous review topics, we'll work through a couple of examples. If you want a more comprehensive review, stop by office hours and check out [this video on adding and subtracting rational expressions](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"} and [this one on simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}

**Problem 7:** Add the two rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x}{6} + \frac{x}{15} &= \frac{x}{2\left(3\right)} + \frac{x}{5\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{5x}{5\left(2\right)\left(3\right)} + \frac{2x}{2\left(5\right)\left(3\right)}\\
&= \frac{5x}{30} + \frac{2x}{30}\\
&= \frac{5x + 2x}{30}\\
&= \boxed{~\frac{7x}{30}~}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 8:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 8:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3} &= \frac{\color{blue}{\left(x + 3\right)}\left(x + 5\right)}{\color{blue}{\left(x + 3\right)}\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{\color{blue}{\left(x + 1\right)}\left(x - 2\right)}{\color{blue}{\left(x + 1\right)}\left(x + 3\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 8:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3} &= \frac{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 5\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x + 3\right)}\\
&= \frac{x^2 + 8x + 15}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{x^2 - x - 2}{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x + 3\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 8:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3} &= \frac{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 5\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x + 3\right)}\\
&= \frac{x^2 + 8x + 15}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{x^2 - x - 2}{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x + 3\right)}\\
&= \frac{\left(x^2 + 8x + 15\right) - \left(x^2 - x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 8:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x + 5}{x+1} - \frac{x - 2}{x + 3} &= \frac{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 5\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x + 3\right)}\\
&= \frac{x^2 + 8x + 15}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)} - \frac{x^2 - x - 2}{\left(x + 1\right)\left(x + 3\right)}\\
&= \frac{\left(x^2 + 8x + 15\right) - \left(x^2 - x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)}\\
&= \boxed{~\frac{9x + 17}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x + 1\right)}~}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}\\
&= \left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}\\
&= \left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{1}{2x}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}\\
&= \left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{1}{2x}\\
&= \frac{x}{2x\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}\\
&= \left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{1}{2x}\\
&= \frac{x}{2x\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}\\
&= \frac{x - \left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}\\
&= \left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{1}{2x}\\
&= \frac{x}{2x\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}\\
&= \frac{x - \left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}\\
&= \frac{-2}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions III

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 9:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2}}$

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}}{2} &= \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)}\\
&= \left(\frac{1}{x + 2} - \frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{1}{2x}\\
&= \frac{x}{2x\left(x + 2\right)} - \frac{\left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}\\
&= \frac{x - \left(x + 2\right)}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}\\
&= \frac{-2}{2x\left(x + 2\right)}\\
&= \boxed{~\frac{-1}{x\left(x + 2\right)}~}
\end{align}

</div>

## Rational Expressions Practice

:::{.fragment}

Try each of the following practice problems.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It 10:** Add the rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{x}{4} + \frac{x}{6}}$

**Try It 11:** Subtract the rational expressions $\displaystyle{\frac{2x}{9} - \frac{x}{6}}$

**Try It 12:** Simplify the rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x - 4}{x + 2} - \frac{x + 1}{x - 3}}$

**Try It 13:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{x + 3}}{2x}}$

**Try It 14:** Simplify the following rational expression $\displaystyle{\frac{\frac{1}{x + h} - \frac{1}{x}}{h}}$

:::

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

One of the reasons factoring is such a critical skill is because it allows us to simplify expressions significantly and gives great insight into the expressions.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Nearly every time we see an expression with exponents in it, we'll want to factor it because the factored form allows us to immediately see when that expression is $0$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

Again, we'll look at a few examples, but for more comprehensive discussions please stop by office hours and check out [this video covering simplification of rational expressions](https://youtu.be/7Uos1ED3KHI?si=2jUtaKinE0KPiAao){target="_blank"}.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 10:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x}}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.* 

\begin{align} \frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

One of the reasons factoring is such a critical skill is because it allows us to simplify expressions significantly and gives great insight into the expressions.

Nearly every time we see an expression with exponents in it, we'll want to factor it because the factored form allows us to immediately see when that expression is $0$.

Again, we'll look at a few examples, but for more comprehensive discussions please stop by office hours and check out [this video covering simplification of rational expressions](https://youtu.be/7Uos1ED3KHI?si=2jUtaKinE0KPiAao){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 10:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x}}$.

*Solution.* 

\begin{align} \frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x} &= \frac{8x}{2x\left(x^2 - 4\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

One of the reasons factoring is such a critical skill is because it allows us to simplify expressions significantly and gives great insight into the expressions.

Nearly every time we see an expression with exponents in it, we'll want to factor it because the factored form allows us to immediately see when that expression is $0$.

Again, we'll look at a few examples, but for more comprehensive discussions please stop by office hours and check out [this video covering simplification of rational expressions](https://youtu.be/7Uos1ED3KHI?si=2jUtaKinE0KPiAao){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 10:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x}}$.

*Solution.* 

\begin{align} \frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x} &= \frac{8x}{2x\left(x^2 - 4\right)}\\
&= \frac{4}{x^2 - 4}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions I

<div style="font-size:17pt">

One of the reasons factoring is such a critical skill is because it allows us to simplify expressions significantly and gives great insight into the expressions.

Nearly every time we see an expression with exponents in it, we'll want to factor it because the factored form allows us to immediately see when that expression is $0$.

Again, we'll look at a few examples, but for more comprehensive discussions please stop by office hours and check out [this video covering simplification of rational expressions](https://youtu.be/7Uos1ED3KHI?si=2jUtaKinE0KPiAao){target="_blank"}.

**Problem 10:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x}}$.

*Solution.* 

\begin{align} \frac{8x}{2x^3 - 8x} &= \frac{8x}{2x\left(x^2 - 4\right)}\\
&= \frac{4}{x^2 - 4}\\
&= \boxed{~\frac{4}{\left(x + 2\right)\left(x - 2\right)}~}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.fragment}

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}
\end{align}

:::

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)}{\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x~?~\text{___}\right)\left(x~?~\text{___}\right)}{\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x - \text{___}\right)\left(x - \text{___}\right)}{\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x - 7\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)\left(\text{___}~?~\text{___}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x - 7\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x~?~\text{___}\right)\left(x~?~\text{___}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x - 7\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x + \text{___}\right)\left(x - \text{___}\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x - 7\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x - 7\right)}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions II

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Problem 11:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21}}$.

*Solution.*

\begin{align} \frac{x^2 - 9x + 14}{x^2 -4x - 21} &= \frac{\left(x - 7\right)\left(x - 2\right)}{\left(x + 3\right)\left(x - 7\right)}\\
&= \boxed{~\frac{x - 2}{x + 3}~}
\end{align}

</div>

## Factoring and Reducing Rational Expressions Practice

:::{.fragment}

Try each of the following practice problems.

:::

:::{.fragment}

**Try It 15:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{6x^2}{3x^3 - 12x^2}}$.

**Try It 16:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{4x + 8}{2x^2 + 8x + 8}}$.

**Try It 17:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - 16}{3x^2 - 12x}}$.

**Try It 18:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{x^2 - x - 6}{x^2 + x - 12}}$.

**Try It 19:** Simplify the expression $\displaystyle{\frac{2x^2 + 5x - 3}{2x^2 - 7x + 3}}$.

:::

## Exit Ticket Task

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="75%"}

Navigate to [our MAT142 Exit Ticket Form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSeUo4K9WBH8xFAVjt3VbJAtkSMimu9DGD7Jv6zPLhZhifCIfQ/viewform?usp=dialog), answer the questions, and complete the *task* below.

<br/>

*Note.* Today's discussion is listed as `1. Algebra Foundation`

:::

:::{.column width="25%"}

![](MAT142_ExitTicket_QR.png)

:::

::::

<div style="font-size:17pt">

**Task:** What can I do to best help you (i) feel more comfortable with me. (ii) feel more comfortable in class, and (iii) ultimately find success in this course and beyond?

</div>

## Summary and Next Time...

<div style="font-size:17pt">

:::{.nonincremental}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

<center>**General Comments**</center>

+ We covered a lot of ground today, and the topics we worked with are genuinely difficult. 
+ Factoring and working with rational expressions are two of the most common places mistakes are made. 
+ Please know that I want to work with you and I want you to be successful in this course.
+ I'm looking forward to working with you in office hours.

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

<center>**Resources for Today's Conent**</center>

+ [Factoring strategies](https://youtu.be/9MUDAr1PnlE?si=WbizeMZeQh6zeibf){target="_blank"}
+ [Rational expressions and common denominators](https://youtu.be/6uJx7PE_ths?si=dKBL6OtoffKzBOYQ){target="_blank"}
+ [Simplification of complex rational expressions](https://youtu.be/ifDEetTq8bM?si=GqKva_upOAsje37l){target="_blank"}
+ [Factoring and simplifying rational expression](https://youtu.be/7Uos1ED3KHI?si=2jUtaKinE0KPiAao){target="_blank"}

:::

::::


:::

:::{.fragment}

<center> **Next Time:** <br/> Describing Intervals of Real Numbers, Function Notation, and Function Evaluation</center>

:::

:::{.fragment}

<center> **Homework:** <br/> Complete Homework 1 on MyOpenMath</center>

:::
